1: τὸν δὲ ἀσθενοῦντα τῇ πίστει προσλαμβάνεσθε — Astheneō (G770, to be weak): the weakness is in tē pistei (in faith). Here pistis (G4102, dl=3) carries a narrower sense than the soteriological pistis of chs. 3–5: it is the personal conviction that enables freedom from scrupulosity regarding food and days. The weak in faith lack this settled conviction, not saving faith. Proslambano (G4355, receive/welcome alongside) — to take someone into one's company, not merely to tolerate them. μὴ εἰς διακρίσεις διαλογισμῶν — not for judging disputed thoughts: diakrisis (G1253) is discrimination/judging; dialogismoi (G1261, calculations, reasonings) — not for the purpose of disputing over their scruples.
2: ὁ μὲν πιστεύει φαγεῖν πάντα — Pisteuei here means believes/is convinced (personal conviction, not saving faith). Eating all things versus eating only vegetables: the background is Jewish dietary law and the difficulty of securing ritually pure meat in Gentile contexts (cf. 1 Cor 8–10). The strong believer's freedom extends to panta (all things); the weak person eats lakhana (G3001, vegetables/herbs).
3: ὁ ἐσθίων τὸν μὴ ἐσθίοντα μὴ ἐξουθενείτω — Exoutheneō (G1848, to treat as nothing, despise): the eater must not despise the abstainer. ὁ δὲ μὴ ἐσθίων τὸν ἐσθίοντα μὴ κρινέτω — the abstainer must not judge (krinō, G2919) the eater. The two wrongs are different: despising (looking down from strength) and judging (condemning from scruple). Both are corrected by the same ground: ὁ θεὸς γὰρ αὐτὸν προσελάβετο — God has welcomed him.
4: σὺ τίς εἶ ὁ κρίνων ἀλλότριον οἰκέτην — Allotrios oiketēs (another person's household servant): to judge another person's slave is to intrude into a master-servant relationship that is not yours. Oiketēs (G3610, house-slave) rather than doulos: the domestic context emphasizes the master's exclusive prerogative of evaluation. τῷ ἰδίῳ κυρίῳ στήκει ἢ πίπτει — he stands or falls before his own master. Stēkō (G4739, stand firm) as perfect of histēmi: present standing. σταθήσεται δέ — he will stand: the divine passive asserts God's capacity to make the servant stand despite the skepticism of the judging party.
5: ὃς μὲν κρίνει ἡμέραν παρ᾽ ἡμέραν, ὃς δὲ κρίνει πᾶσαν ἡμέραν — The day-observance dispute: one person judges (discerns, considers, G2919) one day above another; another judges every day alike. The background likely includes Sabbath and feast-day observance. ἕκαστος ἐν τῷ ἰδίῳ νοῒ πληροφορείσθω — each person be fully persuaded in their own mind (plērophoreō, G4135, to fully convince, fill with certainty). Nous (G3563) is the moral reasoning faculty. The responsibility for conviction rests with each individual before God, not with the community's consensus.
6: ὁ φρονῶν τὴν ἡμέραν κυρίῳ φρονεῖ — Phronō (G5426, to think, to regard with mindful attention) — the one observing the day observes it to the Lord. The parallel construction: eating/abstaining, both directed to the Lord with thanksgiving (eukharisteō, G2168). The criterion for both is the same: orientation toward the Lord and giving of thanks. The content of the practice differs; the Lord-directedness does not.
7: οὐδεὶς γὰρ ἡμῶν ἑαυτῷ ζῇ καὶ οὐδεὶς ἑαυτῷ ἀποθνῄσκει — The principle underlying the Lord-orientation of v.6: no one lives to themselves, no one dies to themselves. Heautō (dative of benefit — for himself alone): life and death are not self-enclosed transactions. The verse is a general anthropological claim before the Christological ground is given in vv.8–9.
8: ἐάν τε γὰρ ζῶμεν, τῷ κυρίῳ ζῶμεν, ἐάν τε ἀποθνῄσκωμεν, τῷ κυρίῳ ἀποθνῄσκομεν — The dative tō kyriō in all four clauses: to the Lord we live, to the Lord we die. Eante (whether) creates the exhaustive disjunction. ἐάν τε οὖν ζῶμεν ἐάν τε ἀποθνῄσκωμεν, τοῦ κυρίου ἐσμέν — the conclusion: we are the Lord's. The genitive tou kyriou marks ownership/belonging — existence in either state is possession by the Lord.
9: εἰς τοῦτο γὰρ Χριστὸς ἀπέθανεν καὶ ἔζησεν — The aorist-then-aorist: apethanen (died) and ezēsen (lived/came to life — a constative aorist for the resurrection). The purpose: ἵνα καὶ νεκρῶν καὶ ζώντων κυριεύσῃ — so that he might be Lord of both dead and living. The scope of Christ's lordship is total: death and resurrection together establish his dominion over all human states. The present-tense ethical claim of ch. 14 is grounded in this Christological fact.
10: σὺ δὲ τί κρίνεις τὸν ἀδελφόν σου; ἢ καὶ σὺ τί ἐξουθενεῖς τὸν ἀδελφόν σου — Two questions — one to the judger, one to the despiser (reversing the order of v.3 to address each party directly). Both actions are delegitimized by the ground that follows: πάντες γὰρ παραστησόμεθα τῷ βήματι τοῦ θεοῦ — all of us will stand before God's judgment seat (bēma, G968, the raised platform of judgment). The plurality and the universality (pantes) undercut the presumption of any individual's pre-judgment of another.
11: γέγραπται γάρ — The citation is from Isa 45:23 (LXX), here blended with Isa 49:18. The perfect gegraptai (it stands written): the scriptural authority is invoked as permanent. Zō egō, legei kyrios — the divine oath formula ("As I live, says the Lord"): God's own life is the pledge. ἐμοὶ κάμψει πᾶν γόνυ καὶ πᾶσα γλῶσσα ἐξομολογήσεται τῷ θεῷ — every knee will bow, every tongue will confess. At Phil 2:10–11 Paul applies the same text to Jesus; here it grounds accountability to God as judge. The application is consistent: the one before whom every knee bows is the one before whose judgment seat all will stand.
12: ἄρα οὖν ἕκαστος ἡμῶν περὶ ἑαυτοῦ λόγον δώσει τῷ θεῷ — Ara oun (therefore then): the logical conclusion from vv.10–11. Logos dōsei (will give account): the forensic idiom — rendering an accounting before a court. Peri heautou (about himself): the account is individual and personal. The implication: since each person must give account for himself, judging another's account in advance is a category error — it presumes standing the judge does not possess.
13: μηκέτι οὖν ἀλλήλους κρίνωμεν — The hortatory subjunctive shifts to communal resolution: let us no longer judge one another. ἀλλὰ τοῦτο κρίνατε μᾶλλον — rather, decide (krinate, aorist imperative of krinō) this: a playful inversion of the prohibition, using the same verb. τὸ μὴ τιθέναι πρόσκομμα τῷ ἀδελφῷ ἢ σκάνδαλον — not to place a stumbling-block (proskomma, G4348) or a trap (skandalon, G4625, the trigger-stick of an animal trap, then metaphorically an occasion for moral failure).
14: οἶδα καὶ πέπεισμαι ἐν κυρίῳ Ἰησοῦ ὅτι οὐδὲν κοινὸν δι᾽ ἑαυτοῦ — Oida (know) + pepeismai (perfect passive of peithō, to persuade — am fully persuaded, G3982): Paul asserts personal conviction at the highest level. Koinos (G2839, common/unclean) — in the ritual purity sense. The principle: nothing is unclean by its own nature (di᾽ heautou). εἰ μὴ τῷ λογιζομένῳ τι κοινὸν εἶναι, ἐκείνῳ κοινόν — but for the one who considers it unclean, it is unclean. Logizomenō (G3049, to reckon, count, consider): the act of personal moral reckoning determines the category for that person. The principle respects the primacy of conscience.
15: εἰ γὰρ διὰ βρῶμα ὁ ἀδελφός σου λυπεῖται — Brōma (G1033, food) is the material object; lypeō (G3076, to grieve, distress) describes the effect on the brother. οὐκέτι κατὰ ἀγάπην περιπατεῖς — you are no longer walking according to love: kata agapēn (according to love) is the governing principle of conduct. μὴ τῷ βρώματί σου ἐκεῖνον ἀπόλλυε ὑπὲρ οὗ Χριστὸς ἀπέθανεν — the escalation is stark: destroying (apollye, G622) someone for whom Christ died — over food. The relative clause hyper hou Christos apethanen (for whom Christ died) grounds the prohibition in the infinite value of the person.
16: μὴ βλασφημείσθω οὖν ὑμῶν τὸ ἀγαθόν — Blasphēmeitō (G987, spoken of as evil): the freedom of the strong, which is genuinely good (to agathon), must not become an occasion for the outsider's blasphemy. The "good" being blasphemed is the liberty in Christ; the behavior of the strong, if exercised without regard for the weak, turns a gift into a scandal.
17: οὐ γάρ ἐστιν ἡ βασιλεία τοῦ θεοῦ βρῶσις καὶ πόσις — Basileia tou theou (kingdom of God): the governing rule of God in its present experiential expression. The kingdom's substance is not eating and drinking (brōsis kai posis) — not dietary and ritual practice. ἀλλὰ δικαιοσύνη καὶ εἰρήνη καὶ χαρὰ ἐν πνεύματι ἁγίῳ — Dikaiosynē (G1343, dl=4) here is behavioral/moral uprightness within the community, not the forensic justification of chs. 1–8; eirēnē (peace) is communal harmony; khara (joy) is the Spirit-produced delight. En pneumati hagiō (in the Holy Spirit): the Spirit is the sphere in which these qualities exist and the agent who produces them.
18: ὁ γὰρ ἐν τούτῳ δουλεύων τῷ Χριστῷ εὐάρεστος τῷ θεῷ καὶ δόκιμος τοῖς ἀνθρώποις — En toutō douleuōn (serving in this — in righteousness, peace, and joy): the participle picks up the three nouns of v.17. Euarestos tō theō (pleasing to God, G2101) — the same word as 12:1's "acceptable/pleasing to God." Dokimos tois anthrōpois (G1384, approved by people — having passed the test): the life lived in the Spirit's fruits wins the approval of the human community as well as God.
19: ἄρα οὖν τὰ τῆς εἰρήνης διώκωμεν — Ara oun + hortatory subjunctive: the conclusion drives to action. Diōkō (G1377, pursue, chase): the competitive-pursuit word recurs from 12:13. Ta tēs eirēnēs (the things of peace) and ta tēs oikodomēs (the things of building up/edification, G3619): two goal-objects to pursue. Oikodomē (building, edifice) used of community formation is a Pauline technical term for the constructive growth of the community.
20: μὴ ἕνεκεν βρώματος κατάλυε τὸ ἔργον τοῦ θεοῦ — Katalyō (G2647, to tear down, demolish — the building-metaphor in reverse). The work of God (the community, the weak believer) is not to be demolished for food. πάντα μὲν καθαρά — all things are clean (the principle Paul agrees with). ἀλλὰ κακὸν τῷ ἀνθρώπῳ τῷ διὰ προσκόμματος ἐσθίοντι — but it is evil for the person who eats through a stumbling-block: the manner of eating that creates the stumbling-block for another is the evil. The freedom is real; the exercise that harms another is not.
21: καλὸν τὸ μὴ φαγεῖν κρέα μηδὲ πιεῖν οἶνον μηδὲ ἐν ᾧ ὁ ἀδελφός σου προσκόπτει — The infinitives are articular (to mē phagein): the good thing is the concrete action of not eating meat, not drinking wine, not doing anything by which the brother stumbles. The list is practical — wine as well as meat were relevant to the Roman context (Diaspora Jewish practice varied on wine at Gentile tables). The conclusion states the norm positively: abstinence, when it prevents a sibling's fall, is good.
22: σὺ πίστιν ἣν ἔχεις κατὰ σεαυτὸν ἔχε ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ — Pistin hēn ekheis (the faith/conviction you have): the strong person's liberty of conscience is a possession to be kept (kata seauton, between yourself and God, not paraded before the community). μακάριος ὁ μὴ κρίνων ἑαυτὸν ἐν ᾧ δοκιμάζει — blessed is the one who does not judge himself in what he approves: dokimazō (G1381, to test/approve). The blessing is for the person whose conscience (pistis/conviction) is settled enough that self-condemnation does not arise.
23: ὁ δὲ διακρινόμενος ἐὰν φάγῃ κατακέκριται — Diakrinomenos (G1252, the one who doubts/wavers, hesitates between options): the wavering person who eats against their own scruple is condemned (katakrinetai, G2632, perfect passive: has been condemned and the condemnation stands). The condemnation is self-condemnation through conscience: ὅτι οὐκ ἐκ πίστεως — because not from faith/conviction. πᾶν δὲ ὃ οὐκ ἐκ πίστεως ἁμαρτία ἐστίν — everything not from faith is sin. Here pistis means the personal settled conviction that makes an action conscientiously permissible for the person doing it. The maxim is not about saving faith but about the relationship between conscience and action: acting against one's own conscience is sin, regardless of the objective permissibility of the act.
